reason: The paper explicitly computes impurity based importance (mean decrease in Gini and times_a_root via randomForestExplainer, with ranger splitrule = gini) and uses it to declare which specific CpG loci are the "most important predictors" ("6 of the 7 (86%) INA, 2 of the 4 (50%) NHLH2, and 2 of the 7 (29%) THBS4"). This ranking directly underwrites a stated translational conclusion in the Discussion: that "a subset of 10 candidate loci from the total of 18 ... already permits the identification of tissue samples with a metastatic disease state ... with good accuracy," framing a minimal biomarker panel. The only validation offered is the 0.73 test-set accuracy of the full 18-locus random forest model; no separate model was fit or tested on just the 10 "important" loci, and no permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance was used to corroborate which loci matter or their rank order. Per the rubric, overall model accuracy on the full feature set does not validate a ranking-based feature subset, so the ranking underlying the 10-of-18 loci claim is uncorroborated and the flag is confirmed.
